title: Best Bullet Journal Supplies of 2026: No More Tiny Kits or Peeling Tape
excerpt: Find bullet journal supplies that actually stick and fit your A5 notebook—skipping tiny kits, flimsy stencils, and tape that peels off.
author: Editorial Team
metaDescription: Find bullet journal supplies that actually stick and fit your A5 notebook—skipping tiny kits, flimsy stencils, and tape that peels off.
intro:
If you've ever ended up with washi tape that lifts off the page by day two or a 'complete' kit the size of a postage stamp, you've stumbled into the bullet journal supply trap. Many budget-friendly products promise variety but skip the fundamentals: reliable adhesion, paper that can handle ink, and sizes that match standard A5 spreads.

This guide cuts past the packaging fluff to focus on supplies that hold up to daily use. We've sorted options that deliver where it counts—notebook paper that resists bleed, stencils that trace cleanly, stickers that stay put—and flagged the trade-offs you'll actually feel. That way, your supplies work with your creative flow, not against it.

products.label: feela Dotted Journal Kit
products.badge: Best Overall Bullet Journal Starter Kit
products.oneLiner: All-in-one A5 kit with 15 pens, stencils, and washi to start bullet journaling immediately.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Test each pen on a scrap page before committing to a layout; lighter pressure reduces bleed risk.
pros: Complete starter bundle includes notebook, pens, stencils, tape, and stickers in one purchase — 82 mentions, 100% positive — praised as perfect starter set
Hardcover notebook with 224 dotted pages feels sturdy for daily journaling — 161 mentions, 93% positive
Pens produce fine lines suitable for intricate details and handwriting — 73 mentions, 95% positive
Cost-effective kit that also works as a packaged gift set — 68 mentions, 87% positive; 61 mentions, 97% positive as gift

Thin plastic stencils may bend during use; some units show torn pages from packaging — 52 mentions, 33% negative — quality control variance
products.verdict: This kit removes the guesswork of assembling a bullet journal starter set, but the ink bleed and stencil flimsiness are boundaries to know before buying.
products.review:
The feela Journal Kit arrives with a hardcover A5 notebook (224 dotted pages, 100 GSM paper), 15 fineliner pens, 5 stencils, 6 sticker sheets, 6 washi tapes, and a black pen. Everything fits in one box, so beginners don't need to shop around for individual supplies. The notebook feels solid and lies reasonably flat when open.

The included fineliners produce fine, consistent lines on the notebook's paper, but occasional ghosting or bleed-through can occur — especially with heavy ink application or thicker marker tips. The stencils are thin PET plastic; they work for basic shapes but can bend with pressure, and some kits have arrived with minor page tears from packaging. These are tradeoffs at this price point.

This set suits someone buying their first bullet journal who wants a single package to start with. Gift-givers will appreciate the attractive packaging and clear starter focus. Skip it if you already own quality pens or stencils, or if you need paper that handles wet media without bleed.

💡 💡 Tip: Test each pen on a scrap page before committing to a layout; lighter pressure reduces bleed risk.

products.label: LEUCHTTURM1917 Official Bullet Journal Edition 2

products.verdict: Dedicated bullet journalers who want the official framework and can live without bundled accessories gain a durable, well-designed notebook. The price is steep for dabblers, but the paper quality and built-in guides justify it for daily practitioners.
products.review:
While bundled journaling kits offer a grab-and-go solution, the LEUCHTTURM1917 Edition 2 focuses solely on the notebook itself, delivering the official Bullet Journal system in a single volume. The numbered pages, pre-printed index, and future log remove the setup grunt work, letting you jump straight into daily logging. The 120 GSM paper is the real anchor — it handles gel pens, fineliners, and most fountain pen inks without ghosting, a step up from typical thin journal papers.

This notebook hits its stride for committed bullet journalers who have already settled on their pens and decorative tools. The pre-structured sections suit those following the Ryder Carroll method precisely; if you prefer a completely blank canvas, the built-in guides may feel restrictive. The premium price reflects the paper quality and official branding, and while stray ink bleed is rare, very wet fountain pen inks can occasionally seep through. That makes it a workhorse for consistent methodology, not a casual doodle pad.

💡 💡 Tip: If you use fountain pens with free-flowing ink, test yours on the rear page — switching to a drier ink or fine gel pen eliminates any bleed-through concern.

The set suits students and planners who want more than the standard 10-color packs but don't need waterproof or archival properties.

Budget-conscious bullet journalers who need a manageable collection of standard shapes without rigid, heavy-duty plastic will find this set practical. Bible journalers and small-craft enthusiasts will appreciate the size and selection, as long as they use a light touch with markers. Those looking for thick, unbendable stencils for aggressive inking will want a heavier gauge alternative.

💡 💡 Tip: Use with light pressure and ballpoint pens or fine liners to avoid bending the thin plastic.

The thin metal can feel less robust than expected, though it withstands bookmark duty without issue.

buyingGuide.heading: Paper Weight & Bleed Resistance
buyingGuide.body:
Not all dot-grid paper handles ink the same way. A 80 GSM sheet acts like a sponge with wet fineliners or markers, allowing ink to seep through to the back. Stepping up to 100 GSM dramatically reduces bleed-through, while 120 GSM nearly eliminates it.

Layering matters too: a heavy coating of wet ink on a 100 GSM page can still ghost. If you use brush pens or gel pens that lay down a lot of ink, prioritize 120 GSM. Casual pencil or ballpoint users can get away with 100 GSM without issue.

buyingGuide.heading: Pen Tip Size & Ink Properties
buyingGuide.body:
Fine-point fineliners (0.38 mm) excel at tiny hand-lettering and habit trackers but rely on water-based ink that isn't waterproof. That means sweat or a spilled drink can smear your work. Going to a larger 0.5 mm gel tip gives bolder lines but demands more from the paper.

The hidden trade-off is ink drying time: fast-drying inks prevent smudging but may clog tips if left uncapped, while slower-drying ink stays wet and risks accidental smears. Test on a back page before committing to a full spread.

buyingGuide.heading: Stencil Material & Design Variety
buyingGuide.body: Plastic stencils feel flexible on purpose, but thinner PET sheets bend under marker pressure, producing imprecise outlines. A 0.3 mm difference in thickness can mean crisp icons versus fuzzy smudges that ruin a layout. Look for reinforced edges or slightly rigid plastic if you frequently use darker markers.

buyingGuide.heading: Washi Tape Adhesion & Design
buyingGuide.body: Repositionable washi is gentle, but that low tack means it can peel up from paper with a bit of handling, especially along the edges of a journal that gets flipped often. Tapes marketed as 'decorative' typically favor easy removal over permanence. If you want tapes to stay put through a year of use without lifting, plan to seal edges with a glue stick or opt for a semi-permanent adhesive roll.

buyingGuide.heading: Sticker Size & Adhesive Reliability
buyingGuide.body: Stickers with lots of tiny words look charming on the sheet, but once placed, small font sizes can become unreadable—especially gold foil on light backgrounds. Weak backing adhesive also means corners curl when you close the notebook repeatedly. Before buying a giant word pack, check that the sticker size is large enough for your layout scale and that reviews don't mention persistent edge lift.

faq.question: Do bullet journal starter kits actually include everything I need, or will I have to buy more supplies?
faq.answer: Most starter kits bundle a notebook, a few fineliners, stencils, and some decorative tape or stickers—enough to create basic layouts right away. However, the included pens may bleed through the journal's own pages, and stencils are often thin plastic. You'll likely add a higher-quality black pen and a backup glue stick for washi that peels, but the kit eliminates the initial hunt.

faq.question: Why does my washi tape keep peeling off my journal pages?
faq.answer: Repositionable washi tape uses a mild adhesive designed to be removed without residue, which also means it can lift with handling—especially on smooth or coated paper. Applying a light layer of glue stick under the tape or sealing edges with clear matte tape creates a more permanent hold. For heavily handled journals, look for washi labeled 'semi-permanent' or plan to reapply adhesive.

faq.question: How can I stop fineliner pens from bleeding through my bullet journal paper?
faq.answer: Pair fine-point (0.38 mm) fineliners with paper that is at least 100 GSM, and avoid prolonged pressure that forces excess ink into the fibers. Let the ink dry fully before closing the notebook to prevent transfer. If bleed-through persists, switch to pigment-based or waterproof fineliners, which tend to sit on the surface rather than soaking in.

faq.question: Are metal stencil bookmarks actually useful for bullet journaling, or are they too small?
faq.answer: The stencil openings on a bookmark-style metal stencil are cut to fit ultra-fine mechanical pencil leads, not standard 0.5 mm gel pens or markers, so tracing clean designs with most journaling pens is frustrating. They work best as a portable ruler and page marker, but as a primary stencil they limit your tool choices. For full icons and layouts, a dedicated plastic stencil set gives far more usable shapes.
